evidence.snippet: gastrointestinal manifestations including gastroesophageal reflux and constipation

evidence.reference_title: Phenotypic features in MECP2 duplication syndrome: Effects of age.
evidence.snippet: Twenty-two percent (n = 15 of 69 total) of the participants had regressed at the time of their visit, and average age of regression was 10.38 years

evidence.reference_title: Phenotypic features in MECP2 duplication syndrome: Effects of age.
evidence.snippet: At baseline visit 28 participants had epilepsy. The average age of seizure onset was 8.96 years

evidence.reference_title: MECP2 Duplication Syndrome.
evidence.snippet: early-onset hypotonia, feeding difficulty, gastrointestinal manifestations including gastroesophageal reflux and constipation

evidence.reference_title: MECP2 Duplication Syndrome.
evidence.snippet: delayed psychomotor development leading to severe intellectual disability

evidence.reference_title: A brief history of MECP2 duplication syndrome: 20-years of clinical understanding.
evidence.snippet: The key features of MDS include intellectual disability, developmental delay, hypotonia, seizures, recurrent respiratory infections, gastrointestinal problems, behavioural features of autism and dysmorphic features

nodes.description: MECP2 overexpression is associated with immunodeficiency characterized by IgA and IgG2 deficiency, low antibody titers against pneumococci, and elevated acute-phase responses. This leads to recurrent and often life-threatening respiratory infections, particularly with encapsulated bacteria. The IRAK1 gene, encoding interleukin-1 receptor-associated kinase 1, is frequently co-duplicated with MECP2, but IRAK1 duplication does not increase canonical NF-kB-induced inflammation at baseline, suggesting the immune dysfunction mechanism is more complex than simple innate immune pathway overactivation.

evidence.reference_title: Infectious and immunologic phenotype of MECP2 duplication syndrome.
evidence.snippet: increased susceptibility to infections in MECP2 duplication syndrome is associated with IgA/IgG2-deficiency, low antibody titers against pneumococci and elevated acute-phase responses

evidence.reference_title: Infectious and immunologic phenotype of MECP2 duplication syndrome.
evidence.snippet: 17/27 analyzed patients suffered from pneumonia, 5/27 from at least one episode of sepsis. Encapsulated bacteria (S.pneumoniae, H.influenzae) were frequently isolated.

evidence.reference_title: IRAK1 Duplication in MECP2 Duplication Syndrome Does Not Increase Canonical NF-κB-Induced Inflammation.
evidence.snippet: most patients with MECP2/IRAK1 duplication syndrome present with recurrent and severe infections, accompanied by strong inflammation. Respiratory infections are the most common cause of death.

evidence.reference_title: IRAK1 Duplication in MECP2 Duplication Syndrome Does Not Increase Canonical NF-κB-Induced Inflammation.
evidence.snippet: Patients with MECP2/IRAK1 duplication syndrome do not show increased canonical NF-κB signaling in immortalized fibroblasts, PBMCs, and whole blood.

evidence.reference_title: MECP2 Duplication Syndrome.
evidence.snippet: MECP2 duplication syndrome is a severe neurodevelopmental disorder characterized by early-onset hypotonia

nodes.description: Duplication of the Xq28 region leads to overexpression of MECP2 protein, a multi-functional protein required for proper brain development and maintenance of brain function. MECP2 is dosage-sensitive; even a relatively modest increase causes severe neurological disease. Overexpression leads to neuronal death in specific brain regions including cortex, hippocampus, cerebellum, and spinal cord. Astrocytic dysfunction, including breakdown of glutamate homeostatic mechanisms, likely contributes to neuronal death.

evidence.reference_title: MECP2 and the biology of MECP2 duplication syndrome.
evidence.snippet: MDS is a neurodegenerative disorder in which neurons die in specific parts of the central nervous system, including the cortex, hippocampus, cerebellum, and spinal cord. Neuronal death likely results from astrocytic dysfunction, including a breakdown of glutamate homeostatic mechanisms.

evidence.reference_title: The MECP2 duplication syndrome.
evidence.snippet: MECP2 duplication syndrome is 100% penetrant in affected males and is associated with infantile hypotonia, severe to profound mental retardation, autism or autistic features, poor speech development, recurrent infections, epilepsy, progressive spasticity, and, in some cases, developmental regression.

evidence.reference_title: The MECP2 duplication syndrome.
evidence.snippet: poor speech development

evidence.reference_title: Phenotypic features in MECP2 duplication syndrome: Effects of age.
evidence.snippet: Motor Dysfunction subscale (Figure 2) scores worsen with increasing age

evidence.reference_title: MECP2 Duplication Syndrome.
evidence.snippet: recurrent respiratory infections (in ~75% of affected individuals)

evidence.reference_title: Antibiotic Prophylaxis, Immunoglobulin Substitution and Supportive Measures Prevent Infections in MECP2 Duplication Syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Respiratory infections are the main cause of early death in patients with MECP2 duplication syndrome.

evidence.reference_title: The MECP2 duplication syndrome.
evidence.snippet: MECP2 duplication syndrome is 100% penetrant in affected males and is associated with infantile hypotonia, severe to profound mental retardation
